documents.source: Petition from two local-level government officials in the district of Rong, Guangxi Province, southern China, to the provincial governor, circa 1850 Our dynasty has always followed the teachings of the ancient sages and, as a result, everyone in our
documents.content:
district
lived in harmony for a long time. As the population increased, resources were plentiful. However, in 1846
local bandits and rebels began attacking our district. They captured the district capital and took government
officials as prisoners. Many people were killed, houses were left in ashes, and farmers’ fields were thick with
weeds. The rebels forced the people to pay land taxes to them. They used official seals to issue false orders to
the population. It was intolerable to see these criminals seize control of the local government.
Your Excellency, last year you promised to send an army to suppress the rebels. We beg you, please have the
army come immediately to exterminate the rebels and save the people. Our local militia has been fighting
them for a long time and we fear that if the militia collapses, the rebels will run free and it will become
impossible for the government to control them.

documents.source: Karl Marx, German political economist, “Revolution in China and in Europe,” article published in the New York Daily Tribune, 1853
documents.content:
The current formidable revolution that is taking place in China1 has unquestionably been caused by the
British cannon forcing upon China the unlimited importation of opium. Faced with the might of the British
arms, the authority of the Manchu [Qing] dynasty fell to pieces and China’s complete isolation from the
civilized world came to an end.
The opium trade changed the balance of trade from being continually in favor of the Chinese to being an
exhausting drain on the silver reserves of the empire. Hence, the emperor made strong decrees against the
opium trade, which were subsequently not enforced. The bribery connected with opium smuggling has
entirely corrupted the Chinese state officers in the southern provinces and thereby undermined the authority
of the state.
The introduction of English mass-produced textiles has had a similar effect on the native Chinese industry to
that on the Ottoman Empire, Persia, and India. In China the spinners and weavers have suffered greatly under
this foreign competition, and their communities have become destabilized as a result.
1 a reference to the Taiping Rebellion

documents.source: Qing China’s Foreign Office, policy letter addressed to all Chinese embassies abroad, 1878 Under the Treaties of Tianjin,1 foreigners in China are not subject to the jurisdiction of the Chine
documents.content:
se imperial
authorities. If they have disputes among themselves, their own consuls in China are to settle them; if they
commit a crime in China, their own diplomats are to punish them according to their national laws. But in
practice, foreigners claim much more than this: they interpret the treaties to mean that they may violate
Chinese laws without consequences. To this we cannot agree—China never gave foreigners permission to
disregard our laws.
A special case of this issue is the missionary question. By the terms of the treaties, China had to agree to
admit Western missionaries and to guarantee them protection. But among the missionaries there are some
who act as if their missions are outside of government control, and among their Chinese converts there are
some who seem to believe being Christians allows them to break the laws of their own country. We cannot
accept this. Chinese subjects, whether Christians or not, must obey completely the laws of China.
1 Signed in 1858, the treaties gave European countries and Japan legal and economic advantages in their relationship with
Qing China.

documents.source: Village elders in the district of Caozhou, Shandong Province, northeastern China, report to the provincial government, 1896
documents.content:
In our district, the wealthy landowners grow richer each year, while the poor have nothing. These rich folks
treat the poor like strangers—they will lend them neither cloth nor grain. They treat their hired laborers
particularly cruelly, arousing a hatred so strong that the poor people are easily tempted to turn to a life of
banditry.
The bandits come every few weeks to people’s houses, bearing sharp knives or foreign rifles. When they ask
the poor people for money at knifepoint, how can the poor refuse? If the grain of the district were more
equally distributed, there would be enough to eat, but there are many without any land. All the poor can do is
sell their labor or turn to a life of crime. In North China a hired farmworker can find work for three months
per year at the most. A person cannot make a living on that. So, when they see the easy lives of the bandits
the people are tempted to join them.

documents.source: Chinese Alliance Association, a coalition of political organizations of young Chinese men studying in foreign countries, “Revolutionary Proclamation,” 1907 Since the beginning of China as a nation, we Chinese have governed our own country despite occasiona
documents.content:
l
interruptions. Today, when we raise the righteous standard of revolt in order to expel an alien race [the
Manchu] that has been occupying China, we do no more than our ancestors have done or expected us to do.
The purpose of past revolutions, such as those conducted by the Ming and Taiping, was to restore China to
the Chinese, and nothing else. We, on the other hand, strive not only to expel the ruling aliens but also to
change the political and economic structure of our country. While we cannot describe in detail this new
political and economic structure in this short proclamation, the basic principles behind it are liberty, equality,
and fraternity. The revolutions of yesterday were revolutions by and for the revolutionaries; our revolution, on
the other hand, is a revolution by and for the people.

documents.source: Anonymous Chinese artist, “A Look at China Now and in the Past,” cartoon published in the Shenzhou Ribao (National Herald) newspaper, Shanghai, 1911 Public Domain
documents.content:
In the images, the tiger represents China, and the men represent Western countries.
The images were labeled as follows:
[upper left image]: “China during the Kangxi and Qianlong emperors [seventeenth and eighteenth
centuries],”
[upper right image]: “China during the Xianfeng and Tongzhi emperors [first half of the nineteenth
century],”
[lower left image]: “China today,” and
[lower right image]: “China in the future.”

documents.source: The abdication decree of the child emperor Puyi, issued by the regent empress Longyu on Puyi’s behalf, officially ending the Qing dynasty, 1912 As a consequence of the uprising of the Republican Army, to which different provinces immediately responded, the empire seethed like a boiling cauldron and the people were plunged into utter misery
documents.content:
It is now
evident that the hearts of the majority of the people are in favor of a republican form of government: the
provinces of the south were the first to espouse the cause, and the generals of the north have since pledged
their support. From the preference of the people’s hearts, the Will of Heaven can be seen. How could We then
bear to oppose the will of millions for the glory of one Family? Therefore, observing the tendencies of the
age on the one hand and studying the opinions of the people on the other, We and His Majesty the Emperor
hereby grant the sovereignty to the people and decide in favor of a republican form of constitutional
government.
END OF DOCUMENTS FOR QUESTION 1

parts.prompt: Evaluate the extent to which foreign involvement led to the collapse of the Qing Empire.
parts.scoring_notes: Full rubric: Thesis (1), Contextualization (1), Evidence from Documents (2), Evidence Beyond Documents (1), Sourcing (1), Complexity (1). Total: 7 points.

scoring_points.description: Thesis: Historically defensible thesis/claim about the extent to which foreign involvement led to the collapse of the Qing Empire, with a line of reasoning.

required_elements: Position on the extent of foreign involvement's role in the Qing collapse
Line of reasoning with at least one main argument (or analytic categories)
alternatives.correct_example: The Qing Empire collapsed due to a combination of Western economic and political imperialism and ethnic tensions between the Han and the Manchu.

required_elements: Clear line of reasoning evaluating foreign involvement vs. internal factors
alternatives.correct_example: The Qing Empire was mainly weakened by internal turmoil.

wrong_examples: The Qing Empire gradually weakened over time — no line of reasoning.
The Qing Empire was able to prevent Western powers from influencing Chinese society — not historically defensible.
The Qing Empire collapsed because of foreign involvement — merely restates the prompt.
common_traps: Writing a thesis that merely restates the prompt without taking a position on extent.
Listing factors without establishing a line of reasoning or analytic categories.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Responds to the prompt with a historically defensible thesis/claim that establishes a line of reasoning. The thesis or claim must consist of one or more sentences located in one place, either in the introduction or the conclusion (which may not be limited to the first or last paragraphs). The thesis or claim must identify a relevant development(s) in the period, although it is not required to encompass the entire period.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Empire collapsed because it was unable to match the technological advancements of Western Europe.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning evaluating the topic of the prompt — a defensible position that foreign technological superiority drove Qing collapse.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Empire collapsed due to a combination of Western economic and political imperialism and ethnic tensions between the Han and the Manchu.
sample_responses.commentary: Establishes a line of reasoning with analytic categories — foreign involvement (economic/political imperialism) plus internal ethnic tensions.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Empire was mainly weakened by internal turmoil.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable thesis/claim — establishes a defensible line of reasoning even though brief.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Empire gradually weakened over time.
sample_responses.commentary: Historically defensible but does not establish a line of reasoning or take a position on the extent of foreign involvement.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Empire collapsed because of foreign involvement.
sample_responses.commentary: Merely restates the prompt — no claim beyond what is already asserted in the question.

scoring_points.description: Contextualization: Accurately describe a broader historical context relevant to the collapse of the Qing Empire.

required_elements: Broader historical context relevant to Qing collapse (e.g., Western imperialism in Asia, comparative modernization, Qing-specific background)
More than a passing phrase — requires developed explanation
alternatives.correct_example: Like other non-Western empires in the late nineteenth century, the Qing experienced military defeat and economic pressure from the industrialized West, leading to calls for reform.

required_elements: Context describing Manchu-Han relations or pre-collapse Qing structures
alternatives.correct_example: Because of their Manchu origins, Qing rulers had problems with their legitimacy.

wrong_examples: The first World War affected Chinese territorial holdings — context not relevant to Qing collapse.
The Qing Dynasty was strongly influenced by Confucian forms of government — passing phrase only.
A generic reference to 'China had problems' without elaboration.
common_traps: Providing a true but undeveloped phrase — context must be more than a passing reference.
Describing context that doesn't actually connect to the Qing collapse (e.g., off-topic events).
scoring_points.official_rubric: Describes a broader historical context relevant to the prompt. Context must relate the topic of the prompt to broader historical events, developments, or processes that occur before, during, or continue after the time frame of the question. To earn this point, the context provided must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: Like other non-Western empires in the late nineteenth century, the Qing experienced military defeat and economic pressure from the industrialized West, leading to calls for reform.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes broader context (comparative late-19th-century non-Western experience with Western pressure) and connects to the Qing situation.

sample_responses.response: Because of their Manchu origins, Qing rulers had problems with their legitimacy.
sample_responses.commentary: Minimally acceptable contextualization — describes the Manchu origin issue and ties it to legitimacy problems that influenced collapse.

sample_responses.response: The first World War affected Chinese territorial holdings.
sample_responses.commentary: Context is not relevant to the topic of the prompt (Qing collapse ended in 1912, before WWI).

sample_responses.response: The Qing Dynasty was strongly influenced by Confucian forms of government.
sample_responses.commentary: Passing phrase only — does not describe a developed context or connect to the argument about Qing collapse.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents (1 point): Accurately describe content from at least three documents to address the topic of the Qing Empire's collapse.

required_elements: Accurate description (not just quotation) of content from at least three documents
Each description must address the topic of Qing collapse
alternatives.correct_example: Document 1's petition shows local government losing control to bandits; Document 2 (Marx) describes British opium and textile imports destabilizing China; Document 5 (Revolutionary Proclamation) shows Han Chinese nationalism calling for expulsion of Manchu rulers.

wrong_examples: Quoting three documents without describing their content or connecting them to the Qing collapse.
Using evidence from fewer than three documents.
Misinterpreting a document (e.g., claiming Document 3 supports extraterritoriality when it opposes abuses of it).
common_traps: Describing documents 'collectively' rather than considering each separately.
Quoting without accompanying description of content.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses the content of at least three documents to address the topic of the prompt. Accurately describe — rather than simply quote — the content from at least three of the documents to address the topic of the collapse of the Qing Empire.

sample_responses.response: Document 4 reports that the poor are treated badly by wealthy landowners and that grain not being distributed equally, combined with government failure, causes people to join local bandits to survive.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline example — accurately describes Document 4's content and connects it to the topic of Qing weakness/collapse.

sample_responses.response: Document 1 (the 1850 petition) shows the Qing state losing control to bandits in Guangxi. Document 2 (Marx) argues British opium imports and mass-produced textiles destabilized the Qing economy and corrupted state officials. Document 7 (Puyi's abdication) confirms the dynasty's political collapse through revolutionary pressure.
sample_responses.commentary: Accurately describes content from three documents, each addressing a different facet of the topic of Qing collapse.

sample_responses.response: The documents talk about problems in China.
sample_responses.commentary: Vague — does not describe content from three specific documents.

scoring_points.description: Evidence from Documents (2nd point): Support an argument using at least six documents.

required_elements: At least six documents used to support an argument
Evidence explicitly connected to the argument
alternatives.correct_example: Using Documents 1, 2, 4 (internal problems), and Documents 3, 5, 6, 7 (foreign/revolutionary pressure) to argue that foreign involvement compounded internal weakness, six documents are used argumentatively, not merely listed.

wrong_examples: Using six documents but merely summarizing them without connecting to an argument.
Using five or fewer documents to support the argument.
common_traps: The six documents do not have to be used in support of a SINGLE argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.
Listing six documents without tying each to a claim in the essay.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Supports an argument in response to the prompt using at least six documents. The six documents do not have to be used in support of a single argument — they can be used across sub-arguments or to address counterarguments.

sample_responses.response: The cartoon (Document 6) demonstrates the change in China from a strong empire that the West feared to a weak empire that was dismantled by the West, implying that foreigners may ultimately bring the empire down.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects the content of Document 6 to an argument about the extent to which foreign involvement led to Qing collapse. Used as part of a six-document argument.

sample_responses.response: The abdication decree (Document 7) explains that it was an internal uprising and a desire for a Republican government that ultimately led to the downfall of the Qing.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — connects Document 7 to the argument about internal drivers of collapse (complementing the foreign-involvement thesis).

sample_responses.response: Documents 2 (Marx on opium/textiles), 3 (extraterritoriality abuses), and 6 (cartoon of China dismembered by the West) demonstrate foreign pressure; Documents 1 (local bandits), 4 (rural economic collapse), 5 (Han nationalist proclamation), and 7 (abdication) show internal breakdown. Taken together, all seven documents support an argument that foreign involvement accelerated — but did not alone cause — the internal collapse of the Qing.
sample_responses.commentary: Uses all seven documents (exceeds threshold of six) in service of a multi-part argument about the intersection of foreign and internal drivers.

scoring_points.description: Evidence Beyond Documents (1 point): At least one specific piece of historical evidence beyond the documents relevant to the argument about Qing collapse.

required_elements: Specific (not vague) piece of historical evidence not in the documents
Must be different from the evidence used for contextualization
alternatives.correct_example: Meiji Japan's encroachment on Qing territories, such as Taiwan and the first Sino-Japanese War, demonstrates how foreign powers — not only Western but also other Asian rivals — accelerated Qing territorial loss and prestige collapse.

wrong_examples: Repeating something from the prompt or a document.
A vague phrase like 'there was imperialism' without a named event, person, or policy.
Using the same evidence as contextualization — must be different.
common_traps: Confusing evidence (specific, named) with contextualization (broader trends).
Re-stating a document's content and calling it 'beyond the documents.'
scoring_points.official_rubric: Uses at least one additional piece of specific historical evidence (beyond that found in the documents) relevant to an argument about the prompt. The evidence must be different from the evidence used to earn the point for contextualization, and must be more than a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: The Qing Dynasty had a number of important courtly rivalries which contributed greatly to political instability, for instance, the Empress Dowager Cixi had the Guangxu Emperor poisoned and killed in 1908.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — provides a specific, named piece of evidence (Cixi/Guangxu) not in the documents and connects it to an argument about internal Qing political instability.

sample_responses.response: Protestant and Catholic missions, which increased after the Opium Wars, contributed to cultural and social conflict in China. For instance, Christianity's rising influence contributed to the start of the Boxer Rebellion.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — names a specific post-Opium-War missionary activity and the Boxer Rebellion as evidence outside the documents, connecting to the foreign-involvement argument.

sample_responses.response: The Treaty of Nanjing (1842) imposed after the First Opium War forced China to cede Hong Kong, open five treaty ports, and pay indemnities — an early foreign shock that set the precedent for later unequal treaties referenced in the documents.
sample_responses.commentary: Specific named treaty and consequences not in the documents, connected to the foreign-involvement argument.

sample_responses.response: There was a lot of imperialism in China.
sample_responses.commentary: Too vague — no specific named evidence.

scoring_points.description: Sourcing (1 point): For at least three documents, explain how/why POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument.

required_elements: Explanation (not merely identification) of POV, purpose, situation, or audience
Must cover at least three documents
Each must be connected to an argument about the Qing collapse
alternatives.correct_example: For Document 5, explain how the POV of the Chinese Alliance Association (Han nationalist students abroad influenced by Western liberal ideas) shaped its call for republican overthrow of the Manchu. For Document 4, explain how the historical situation of 1890s rural Shandong (population pressure, inequality) made the banditry problem indicative of systemic Qing weakness. For Document 1, explain how the audience (provincial governor) and purpose (appeal for military aid) reveal the degree to which central authority had eroded.

wrong_examples: Merely identifying POV: 'Marx was a socialist' — does not explain why this matters for an argument.
Summarizing a document's content without addressing POV/purpose/situation/audience.
Sourcing fewer than three documents.
common_traps: Identifying POV without explaining its relevance — the SG repeatedly emphasizes HOW/WHY, not just WHAT.
Summarizing content and calling it sourcing.
scoring_points.official_rubric: For at least three documents, explains how or why the document's point of view, purpose, historical situation, and/or audience is relevant to an argument. Must explain how or why — rather than simply identifying — the POV, purpose, historical situation, or audience is relevant to an argument that addresses the prompt for each of the three documents sourced.

sample_responses.response: The authors [of Document 5] were influenced by Han Chinese nationalism as well as Western liberal political ideas in calling for the overthrow of the Qing Empire and the establishment of a republic.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — identifies the POV of Document 5 (Han nationalist + Western liberal) and connects it to an argument about how internal ideological shifts drove Qing collapse.

sample_responses.response: The cartoon [Document 6] is likely intended to demonstrate how China has become passive and is now being divided up by Westerners.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains the purpose of Document 6 and connects to an argument about foreign dismemberment as a driver of Qing collapse.

sample_responses.response: The report [Document 4] addresses the banditry problem, which was a consequence of population growth and rural poverty in nineteenth-century China.
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains the historical situation of Document 4 and connects to an argument about internal socioeconomic pressure contributing to Qing collapse.

sample_responses.response: Because the petition [Document 1] is an appeal for the provincial government to intervene militarily and restore order, it highlights the damages that bandits have done to government authority in the province (levying taxes on the population, issuing false orders, misusing official seals).
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — explains the purpose and audience of Document 1 and connects it to an argument about erosion of Qing authority.

sample_responses.response: Document 2 was written by Karl Marx.
sample_responses.commentary: Only identifies the author — does not explain how or why Marx's POV is relevant to an argument.

scoring_points.description: Complexity (1 point): Demonstrate a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt.

required_elements: Sustained complex argumentation (not a single concluding sentence)

Ways to earn: analyze multiple variables; explain nuance; explain similarity AND difference, or multiple causes, or causes AND effects; explain cross-period/cross-space connections; corroborate multiple perspectives; qualify/modify by diverse evidence
alternatives.correct_example: Argue that while the Qing Dynasty had to deal with considerable outside threats (Documents 2, 3, 5, 6), it also faced internal problems of deteriorating socioeconomic and political conditions (Documents 2 and 4) and rising Han Chinese nationalism (Documents 5 and 7). The intersection of foreign pressure with internal decay — not foreign pressure alone — explains why reform attempts failed where Meiji Japan's succeeded.

wrong_examples: 'In conclusion, the Qing collapse was complex' — a closing sentence does not demonstrate sustained complexity.
Naming a single complication without integrating it into the argument.
Mentioning another empire without explaining the insightful connection.
common_traps: Complexity at the end of the essay only — SG requires it to be part of the argument throughout.
Listing 'many factors' without showing their interaction or relative importance.
scoring_points.official_rubric: Demonstrates a complex understanding of the historical development that is the focus of the prompt, using evidence to corroborate, qualify, or modify an argument that addresses the question. This demonstration of complex understanding must be part of the argument, not merely a phrase or reference.

sample_responses.response: An argument that while the Qing Dynasty had to deal with considerable outside threats and pressures in the 19th century (Documents 2, 3, 5, and 6), it also faced many problems that related to deteriorating domestic socio-economic and political conditions (Documents 2 and 4) and rising Han Chinese nationalism (Documents 5 and 7).
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — analyzes multiple variables (foreign pressure, internal decay, rising Han nationalism) and explains nuance across documents.

sample_responses.response: Exploring the similarities (foreign concessions and extraterritorial rights, reform attempts frustrated by conservative resistance, nationalist challenges, population pressure) and differences (WWI, territorial collapse of Ottoman Empire due to Arab nationalism and war) between China and the Ottoman Empire as 'semi-sovereign' states that collapsed during the age of Western imperialism. Students could also explore why the Qing collapsed despite reform attempts while Meiji Japan industrialized and became a world power (more homogeneous Japanese population and no population explosion, more Japanese openness to cultural borrowing, effective leadership from the Japanese emperor).
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — demonstrates complexity through insightful cross-empire and cross-regional connections (Qing vs. Ottoman, Qing vs. Meiji Japan).

sample_responses.response: The population explosion faced by the Qing Dynasty (referred to indirectly in Document 4), which initially could be taken as a sign of its success, might have led to the collapse of the dynasty regardless of pressure from foreign powers. The collapse might also be explained as having taken place despite China's successful and rapid modernization in the late 19th and early 20th centuries in response to outside influences, which included new ideas (Document 5), the mass media (Document 6), and a military coup (alluded to in Document 7).
sample_responses.commentary: From the CB guideline — qualifies the main argument by bringing in evidence that complicates or modifies the thesis (population growth as paradoxical driver of collapse).

sample_responses.response: In conclusion, the Qing collapse was complex and had many causes.
sample_responses.commentary: Single closing sentence — does not demonstrate sustained complexity as part of the argument.
